intro:
The doctrine of Christ's three offices (munus triplex) organizes his saving work under the categories of prophet, priest, and king — the three roles in Israel for which individuals were anointed with oil. As prophet, Christ declared God's word with supreme authority: Moses had promised a prophet greater than himself (Deut. 18:15-18), and Jesus fulfilled this in his teaching ministry (Acts 3:22), performing signs authenticating his message, and being himself the divine Word incarnate. The prophetic office extended through the Spirit's ongoing guidance of the church and will be consummated in the final revelation (Rev. 1:1).

As priest, Christ mediated between God and humanity through his sacrifice and intercession. Where the Levitical priests offered animals repeatedly, Christ offered himself once and entered the heavenly sanctuary with his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption (Heb. 9:12, 24). His intercession continues perpetually: 'he always lives to intercede for them' (Heb. 7:25). As king, Christ was installed at the right hand of the Father at his ascension (Ps. 110:1; Acts 2:33-36), exercises dominion over all creation and the church (Eph. 1:20-22; Col. 2:15), and will return to consummate his kingdom over every hostile power (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Rev. 19:11-16). The three offices together provide the comprehensive framework within which Calvin (Institutes 2.15) and subsequent Reformed theology organized the whole of Christ's redemptive work as the fulfillment of Israel's three anointed roles.
